title: Section 2(42A) — Holding period for long-term capital asset (foreign equity)
section: IT Act §2(42A), as amended by Finance (No. 2) Act 2024 s.3
text: Section 2(42A) of the Income Tax Act 1961 defines a 'short-term capital asset' as a capital asset held for not more than a specified period immediately before the date of transfer. For equity shares listed on a recognised stock exchange in India the period is 12 months. For all other capital assets — including shares and securities listed on foreign stock exchanges (NYSE, NASDAQ) which are not 'recognised' under Indian law — the relevant period is 24 months (reduced from 36 months by Finance (No. 2) Act 2024 section 3, effective 23 July 2024). An asset held for MORE THAN 24 months is a long-term capital asset; 24 months or fewer is short-term. The engine computes holding days as (sale_date - acquisition_date).days and compares against threshold_days = 24 × 30 = 720 days.

title: Classification of US-listed equity as unlisted under Indian tax law
text: A 'recognised stock exchange' under IT Act §2(35A) and the Securities Contracts (Regulation) Act 1956 means an Indian exchange notified by SEBI — it does not include NYSE, NASDAQ, or any other foreign exchange. Accordingly, shares of US companies listed on US exchanges are treated as 'unlisted securities' or 'foreign assets' for all Indian capital-gains purposes. This classification drives three consequences: (a) the holding-period threshold for long-term status is 24 months, not 12 months; (b) LTCG is taxed under §112 at 12.5%, not under §112A (which applies only to Indian-listed equity on which STT is paid); (c) STCG is taxed at slab rates, not at the §111A concessional rate. Finance (No. 2) Act 2024 s.7 amending §112(1)(c)(iii) confirmed this treatment expressly.

title: Section 112 — LTCG rate 12.5% on foreign/unlisted equity after Finance Act 2024
section: IT Act §112(1)(c)(iii), substituted by Finance (No. 2) Act 2024 s.7
text: Section 112(1) of the Income Tax Act 1961 taxes long-term capital gains from unlisted securities and foreign equity. Finance (No. 2) Act 2024 section 7, effective 23 July 2024, substituted §112(1)(c)(iii) to impose a flat rate of 12.5% WITHOUT the benefit of indexation. Prior to this amendment the assessee could choose between 20% with indexation or 10% without; that option no longer exists for transfers on or after 23 July 2024. Section 112A (Indian listed equity with STT) provides a distinct 12.5% rate with a ₹1,25,000 annual exemption; §112A does NOT apply to foreign equity. Transitional relief (choice of old rates) exists for land or building acquired before 23 July 2024 only — it does not apply to shares or securities. LTCG tax = LTCG amount × 12.5%.

title: Removal of indexation benefit for unlisted/foreign equity — Finance Act 2024
section: IT Act §48 second proviso; §112(1)(c)(iii) as amended by Finance (No. 2) Act 2024 s.7
text: Section 48 second proviso allows indexation of the cost of acquisition using the Cost Inflation Index (CII) notified annually for long-term capital assets. Finance (No. 2) Act 2024 section 7 amended §112(1)(c)(iii) to apply the 12.5% rate 'without giving effect to the second proviso to §48', thereby withdrawing the indexation benefit for unlisted securities (including US-listed equity treated as unlisted under Indian law) with effect from 23 July 2024. The cost of acquisition is taken at the actual historical INR equivalent converted at the TTBR on the date of acquisition, without CII uplift. No Cost Inflation Index benefit is available for FY 2025-26 LTCG on foreign equity.

title: No annual exemption under Section 112 for LTCG on foreign equity
section: IT Act §112 vs §112A — exemption threshold comparison
text: Section 112A(1), as amended by Finance (No. 2) Act 2024, provides that LTCG exceeding ₹1,25,000 in an assessment year is taxable at 12.5% for equity shares listed on a recognised Indian stock exchange and equity-oriented mutual fund units. This annual threshold of ₹1,25,000 applies ONLY to §112A income. Section 112, which governs LTCG on foreign equity (unlisted under Indian law), contains NO equivalent annual exemption. Every rupee of LTCG from US-listed equity is taxable at 12.5% from the first rupee, without any basic deduction.

title: Rule 115 — TTBR conversion of foreign currency for capital gains
section: IT Rules 1962, Rule 115; IT Act §48 first proviso
text: Rule 115 of the Income Tax Rules 1962 specifies the exchange rate for computing income arising in foreign currency. For capital gains on assets held in foreign currency: (a) sale proceeds in USD are converted to INR at the Telegraphic Transfer Buying Rate (TTBR) of the State Bank of India as on the date of transfer (sale date); (b) cost of acquisition in USD is converted to INR at the TTBR of SBI as on the date of acquisition. The TTBR is the rate at which SBI purchases foreign currency from a customer — the number of Indian Rupees paid per US dollar. Section 48 first proviso mandates this conversion for assets denominated in foreign currency. The cost in INR = (cost in USD cents × TTBR in paise per USD) ÷ 100 (converting paise to rupees). All gains and tax figures in the engine are stored and reported in paise (1 rupee = 100 paise) for integer arithmetic precision.

title: Section 48 — Mode of computation of capital gains
text: Section 48 of the Income Tax Act 1961 provides that capital gains are computed as: Full Value of Consideration MINUS (i) expenditure incurred wholly and exclusively in connection with the transfer (e.g. brokerage commissions, transfer charges) and (ii) cost of acquisition and cost of improvement. The first proviso to §48 requires that where the asset was acquired in foreign currency, the cost of acquisition and the full value of consideration are each computed at the prescribed exchange rate (Rule 115 TTBR). The second proviso (indexation via CII) does not apply to unlisted securities/foreign equity after Finance (No. 2) Act 2024. Net proceeds = (sale price per share × quantity − brokerage commission), all converted at the disposal-date TTBR.

title: STCG on foreign equity taxed at slab rates — Section 111A not applicable
section: IT Act §111A; First Schedule to Finance Act 2025
text: Section 111A of the Income Tax Act applies a concessional flat rate (20% post Finance (No. 2) Act 2024) on STCG from equity shares listed on a recognised Indian stock exchange subject to STT. US-listed equity attracts neither STT nor Indian-exchange listing, so §111A is inapplicable. Short-term capital gains from US-listed equity are included in the assessee's total income and taxed at the applicable income-tax slab rates under Part I of the First Schedule to the Finance Act for the relevant assessment year. For AY 2026-27 (FY 2025-26) under the old regime: nil up to ₹2.5 lakh; 5% from ₹2.5L–₹5L; 20% from ₹5L–₹10L; 30% above ₹10L. The engine receives the marginal slab rate as an explicit parameter from the caller — it does not compute slab rates internally.

title: Section 70(2) — Set-off of short-term capital loss against STCG and LTCG
text: Section 70(2) of the Income Tax Act 1961 provides that where the net result for any assessment year from any source under the head 'Capital gains, being short-term capital gains' is a loss, the assessee shall be entitled to have the amount of such loss set off against income from any other source under the same head (Capital gains) in that year. A short-term capital loss (STCL) may therefore be set off against both short-term capital gains (STCG) and long-term capital gains (LTCG). The engine applies STCL against STCG first (eliminating tax at the higher slab rate), then any remaining STCL against LTCG. This order maximises taxpayer benefit: STCG is taxed at the marginal slab rate (up to 30% + surcharge + cess) while LTCG is taxed at only 12.5% + surcharge + cess — so extinguishing STCG first saves more tax per rupee of loss.

title: Section 70(3) — Long-term capital loss may offset only LTCG, not STCG
text: Section 70(3) of the Income Tax Act 1961 provides that where the net result for any assessment year in respect of any source under the head 'Capital gains, being long-term capital gains' is a loss, such loss shall be set off only against income under the head 'Capital gains, being long-term capital gains'. A long-term capital loss (LTCL) CANNOT be used to offset short-term capital gains. This is asymmetric: STCL offsets both STCG and LTCG (§70(2)), but LTCL offsets only LTCG (§70(3)). A taxpayer with STCG but only LTCL in a year must carry the LTCL forward; it cannot reduce the current-year STCG liability.

title: Section 74 — Carry-forward of capital losses; Section 80 — mandatory timely filing
text: Section 74(1) of the Income Tax Act 1961 provides that unabsorbed capital losses (STCL and LTCL) that cannot be set off in the current year may be carried forward to the following assessment year. The maximum period of carry-forward is eight assessment years immediately succeeding the loss year. Carried STCL may be set off against STCG or LTCG in future years; carried LTCL may be set off only against future LTCG. Section 80 imposes a mandatory condition: losses under Chapter VI (including §74 capital losses) may be carried forward ONLY if the return of income for the loss year was filed before the due date specified under Section 139(1). An assessee who files a belated return under Section 139(4) forfeits the carry-forward right for that year's capital losses. The §139(1) due date for a non-audit individual is 31 July of the assessment year (e.g., 31 July 2026 for AY 2026-27).

title: Surcharge slabs on capital gains — FY 2025-26 (old regime)
section: Finance Act 2023 and Finance Act 2025 — surcharge on resident individuals
text: Surcharge is levied on income-tax payable by a resident individual under the old tax regime for AY 2026-27 at: nil for total income up to ₹50 lakh; 10% for income ₹50L–₹1Cr; 15% for income ₹1Cr–₹2Cr; 25% for income ₹2Cr–₹5Cr; 37% for income above ₹5Cr. CRITICAL DISTINCTION: for LTCG under §112A (Indian listed equity), surcharge on that income is capped at 15% by specific Finance Act provisos. NO such cap exists for LTCG under §112 (foreign/unlisted equity) — the full slab surcharge including 37% applies. STCG taxed at slab rates also attracts the full unrestricted surcharge. Under the new tax regime (§115BAC), maximum surcharge is 25%. Surcharge is computed on total income including non-capital-gains income; the engine receives taxable_income_inr as a parameter and the caller is responsible for supplying the correct total-income figure.

title: Health and Education Cess — 4% on income tax plus surcharge
section: Finance Act 2018 onwards; applicable from AY 2019-20
text: Health and Education Cess at 4% is levied on the aggregate of income-tax and applicable surcharge payable by any taxpayer from AY 2019-20 onwards. The Finance Act 2018 replaced the earlier 3% education cess (2% Education Cess + 1% Secondary and Higher Education Cess) with this unified 4% cess. The rate has remained unchanged from FY 2018-19 through FY 2025-26. Cess is payable on the total income-tax liability including both LTCG tax and STCG tax, after adding surcharge. There is no threshold or exemption from cess. Formula: Cess = 4% × (Income Tax + Surcharge). The cess is added to the total tax liability after surcharge computation.

title: GAAR and wash-sale risk — IT Act §95-102, CBDT Circular 6/2016
section: IT Act §95–§102 (GAAR, effective AY 2018-19); CBDT Circular 6/2016
text: India has no statutory wash-sale rule equivalent to US IRC §1091. A capital loss crystallised by an arm's-length sale is prima facie allowable even if the same security is immediately repurchased. However, two provisions impose risk. First, General Anti-Avoidance Rule (GAAR) under IT Act §95–§102 (applicable from AY 2018-19): the Assessing Officer may disregard or recharacterise any arrangement if its dominant or one main purpose is to obtain a tax benefit and it lacks commercial substance or misuses Act provisions. A same-day or near-term repurchase of the same security to crystallise a loss, with no genuine change in economic position, is a paradigm GAAR target. Second, CBDT Circular 6/2016 specifically targets sham and circular transactions designed to create artificial capital losses. Any repurchase of the same US equity within 30 calendar days of a loss-realising sale must be flagged to the assessee's CA before the loss is claimed. This report does NOT recommend any immediate rebuy.

title: Schedule FA — Mandatory disclosure of foreign assets in ITR-2/ITR-3
section: ITR-2/ITR-3 Schedule FA; IT Act §139 read with Rule 12; Black Money Act 2015
text: Schedule FA (Foreign Assets) is a mandatory schedule in ITR-2 and ITR-3 for resident and ordinarily resident (ROR) individuals who hold any foreign asset at any time during the financial year or have signing authority in any foreign account. US equity holdings, US brokerage accounts (IBKR, Schwab, Fidelity, etc.) and all income from such assets must be disclosed. Required information: country code and ZIP; name and address of the financial institution; account or holding identification number; peak value during the year in foreign currency and INR (converted at TTBR on the last day of the year); income from the asset (dividends, capital gains, interest) that must also appear in the relevant income schedule. Failure to disclose foreign assets in Schedule FA attracts severe penalties under the Black Money (Undisclosed Foreign Income and Assets) and Imposition of Tax Act 2015. FATCA/CRS automatic exchange of information means the Income Tax Department receives US brokerage data directly from US financial institutions.
